subsystem: Flatpak session-helper D-Bus portal: the org.freedesktop.portal.Flatpak `Spawn` method handler (`handle_spawn`). It runs inside the flatpak-portal service (which is privileged/outside the sandbox) and, on request from an already-sandboxed Flatpak application, builds a `flatpak run ...` command line plus an environment block and a set of inherited file descriptors, then launches a new sandboxed instance via g_spawn_async_with_pipes(). It is the code path a sandboxed app uses to start subprocesses.
provenance: Identified by the GLib/GDBus idioms and Flatpak-specific symbols: parameter type `PortalFlatpak *object` and the terminal call `portal_flatpak_complete_spawn (object, invocation, NULL, pid)` (generated GDBus skeleton for the org.freedesktop.portal.Flatpak interface), the `FLATPAK_SPAWN_FLAGS_*` / `FLATPAK_SPAWN_SANDBOX_FLAGS_*` enums, the `FLATPAK_METADATA_GROUP_*`/`FLATPAK_METADATA_KEY_*` keyfile constants, and helpers `flatpak_buffer_to_sealed_memfd_or_tmpfile`, `flatpak_instance_new_for_id`, `glnx_steal_fd`, `GLnxTmpfile`. This is `portal/flatpak-portal.c` from the flatpak project. The in-file banner `/* sample: primevul_c_validation_0305_fixed */` and the corpus filename `train_0100_cwe74.c` indicate a PrimeVul-derived sample labelled CWE-74 (injection), specifically the FIXED variant.
trust_boundary: The trust boundary is the D-Bus method call itself. The CALLER is a Flatpak-sandboxed application; the HANDLER runs with the portal's (host, un-sandboxed) privileges. Therefore every method argument the caller supplies is hostile: `arg_cwd_path`, `arg_argv` (the command and its arguments), `arg_fds` (a{uh} fd handle map), `arg_envs` (a{ss} environment), `arg_flags`, `arg_options` (a{sv} with sandbox-expose, sandbox-flags, sandbox-expose-fd, ...), and the `fd_list` passed alongside. The core danger is INJECTION (CWE-74): any of these strings could try to smuggle extra `flatpak run` options or influence the launcher's own environment, escaping the sandbox. `app_info` (the caller's `.flatpak-info` metadata, group/key values like runtime, branch, arch) is generated by flatpak at sandbox-setup time and is only indirectly caller-influenced, but must still be treated as possibly malformed.

what_the_code_does.code:
handle_spawn (PortalFlatpak         *object,
              GDBusMethodInvocation *invocation,
              GUnixFDList           *fd_list,
              const gchar           *arg_cwd_path,
              const gchar *const    *arg_argv,
              GVariant              *arg_fds,
              GVariant              *arg_envs,
              guint                  arg_flags,
              GVariant              *arg_options)
what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the GDBus-generated signal handler for the org.freedesktop.portal.Flatpak `Spawn` method. `object` and `invocation` are the skeleton and the in-flight method call (used to return replies/errors). `fd_list` carries the actual file descriptors that accompany the D-Bus message. The remaining parameters are the wire arguments produced by the calling sandboxed app: `arg_cwd_path` (working directory), `arg_argv` (NULL-terminated command vector, element 0 is the program), `arg_fds` is an a{uh} GVariant mapping destination-fd -> fd-handle index, `arg_envs` is an a{ss} of environment name/value pairs, `arg_flags` is a bitmask, and `arg_options` is an a{sv} dictionary of extra options. All of these are attacker-controlled.

The local declarations. `flatpak_argv` is the GPtrArray that will accumulate the argv for `flatpak run` (owned strings, freed with g_free). `fd_map` is a heap array of FdMapEntry (allocated later from the caller's fd count) describing how caller fds map into the child. `fds`/`fds_len` will point at the raw descriptor table from `fd_list`. `child_setup_data` is passed to the child-setup callback that performs the dup2s. Many `g_autofree`/`g_auto(GStrv)`/`g_autoptr` locals (app_id, branch, arch, runtime_ref, shares, sockets, devices, sandbox_expose[_ro], ...) will be filled from the caller's app metadata and from arg_options, and are auto-freed on every return path.

The two special child fds are initialized to -1 (sentinel meaning "none"). If a `fd_list` accompanied the call, `g_unix_fd_list_peek_fds` returns a borrowed pointer to the descriptor array and writes its length into `fds_len`. This `fds`/`fds_len` pair is the ONLY source of truth for which descriptors the caller actually passed; every `arg_fds` handle index is later bounds-checked against `fds_len` before dereferencing `fds[handle]`.

`app_info` is the caller's parsed `.flatpak-info` GKeyFile, previously attached to the invocation by the auth layer. It identifies which sandboxed app is calling. `app_id` is read from the [Application] Name key and must be non-empty; an empty id means the caller is not actually running inside a flatpak, which is rejected. This gate establishes that only genuine sandboxed apps reach the rest of the handler.

Input normalization/validation. An empty cwd string is coerced to NULL (meaning "inherit"). `arg_argv` must be non-NULL and have a non-NULL first element or the request is rejected with "No command given". Crucially, `arg_flags` is validated against the whitelist mask `FLATPAK_SPAWN_FLAGS_ALL`: any bit outside the known set causes rejection, so unknown/future flag bits cannot be smuggled in.

The caller's runtime reference is read from [Application] Runtime. It is expected to be a canonical flatpak ref of the form `runtime/ID/ARCH/BRANCH`. Only NULL is checked; the string is then split on '/' into `runtime_parts`. The code later indexes `runtime_parts[1]` and `runtime_parts[3]` assuming exactly (at least) four components exist — but the split result's length is never validated here (see what_could_go_wrong).

A block of reads from the caller's instance metadata: branch, instance_path (the sandbox's per-instance directory, needed to resolve sandbox-expose paths), arch, extra_args (extra flatpak arguments the launcher recorded for this instance), the app/runtime commit ids, and the CONTEXT permission lists shares/sockets/devices. These describe the caller's already-granted permissions and are used below to decide which `--socket=`, `--share=`, `--device=` options are legal to forward. They come from flatpak-generated metadata, not directly from the D-Bus call.

The caller-supplied `arg_options` dictionary is unpacked: string arrays sandbox-expose / sandbox-expose-ro (paths to newly expose into the child's sandbox), a `sandbox-flags` bitmask, and two 'ah' arrays of fd handles to expose. `sandbox_flags` is validated against `FLATPAK_SPAWN_SANDBOX_FLAGS_ALL` whitelist. (Note the error message interpolates `arg_flags`, not `sandbox_flags` — a cosmetic bug in the diagnostic string only.)

Guards for the sandbox-expose feature. If the caller has no instance_path (no per-instance directory to resolve relative expose paths), any non-empty expose list is rejected. Then each requested expose path is validated by `is_valid_expose` (which rejects absolute paths, `..` traversal, etc.); the analogous read-only loop follows at 166-175. This is a path-injection defense: the caller cannot expose arbitrary host paths, only well-formed relative names under its own instance.

The number of fd-map entries is taken from the caller's `arg_fds` variant (only if a real fd array was attached). `fd_map` is heap-allocated zeroed with exactly that many FdMapEntry slots, and pointer+length are handed to `child_setup_data` for the child-setup callback. Because both the loop bound below and the allocation come from the same `n_fds`, the array writes `fd_map[i]` for i<n_fds are in-bounds.

The fd-mapping loop. Each `arg_fds` entry yields `dest_fd` (target fd number in the child, read as the 'u' member) and `handle` (an index into the passed descriptor table, the 'h' member). `handle` is bounds-checked against `[0, fds_len)` BEFORE `fds[handle]` is dereferenced — this is the key defense preventing an out-of-bounds read of the descriptor table via a hostile handle. The resolved real descriptor is stored as `.from`, the requested destination as `.to`/`.final`.

If the caller maps one of the standard descriptors (0/1/2) and the underlying fd is a tty, the first such tty is remembered so the child can adopt it as its controlling terminal (enabling running in a terminal). `max_fd` tracks the highest fd number seen across both `.to` and `.from`, so the conflict-resolution pass below can allocate fresh, non-colliding destination fds above it.

A second pass that detects when a requested destination fd (`to`) collides with a source fd (`from`) still needed by a later entry (j>i). If so it relocates that destination to a fresh number `++max_fd`, so the eventual dup2 sequence in the child does not clobber a descriptor before it has been consumed. This ordering/reuse logic is the hardening added in response to the fd-handling sandbox-escape class (CVE-2021-21261).

The environment block for the child is assembled. `env` starts either empty (if CLEAR_ENV) or from the portal's own environment. Then each caller-supplied name/value pair from `arg_envs` is validated: the NAME must be non-empty and must not contain '=' (so a caller cannot inject `NAME=VALUE\0OTHER=...` splitting). Each accepted pair is appended to `env_string` as `NAME=VALUE\0`, a NUL-delimited blob. Critically these caller values are NOT merged into `env` (the launcher's own environment) — the comment at 260-266 explains they must stay out of flatpak(1)'s environment to avoid code injection into the launcher, and instead be passed to the child via a memfd.

The argv is seeded with the fixed tokens "flatpak" and "run", then the sandboxed vs. non-sandboxed branch is chosen from the SANDBOX flag. In the sandboxed branch, each optional forwarding (--socket=wayland/x11/pulseaudio, --share=ipc, --device=dri, --session-bus, --a11y-bus) is only added when BOTH the caller requested it via a sandbox-flag AND the caller's own already-granted permission lists (sockets/shares/devices from metadata) contain the matching capability. All added tokens are hard-coded constant strings — the caller cannot inject arbitrary values here; it can only toggle among a fixed menu it is already entitled to.

In the non-sandboxed branch, the launcher's recorded `extra_args` are replayed. Any `--env=NAME=VALUE` extra-arg is peeled off and folded into the NUL-delimited `env_string` (after empty-name and missing-value checks) rather than being passed as a literal `--env=` argument; all other extra-args are forwarded verbatim to `flatpak run`. This again routes environment content into the confidential env block instead of the visible command line. `extra_args` originates from flatpak metadata, not the direct D-Bus call.

This is the heart of the CVE-74 mitigation. The accumulated NUL-delimited environment blob is written into a sealed memfd (or tmpfile), and only the integer fd number is placed on the `flatpak run` command line as `--env-fd=%d`. The environment VALUES never appear as command-line text and never enter the launcher's own environment, so a caller cannot use environment content to inject additional flatpak options or to influence flatpak(1) itself; the child reads them from the fd. The fd is stashed in child_setup_data so the child-setup callback can keep it open across the exec.

Handles the EXPOSE_PIDS / SHARE_PIDS flags. It first checks the global `supports` capability (expose-pids is impossible with setuid bwrap) and rejects if unavailable. It resolves the caller's own instance to its pid-namespace leader pid (`sender_pid1`) via the instance id from metadata, requires it to be found, and passes `--parent-pid=%d` plus either `--parent-share-pids` or `--parent-expose-pids`. The pid is derived from the caller's real instance, not from any caller-supplied integer, so it cannot be spoofed to another process.

For NOTIFY_START, a pipe is created; the child writes its new instance id into the write end (passed as `--instance-id-fd`), and the portal reads asynchronously from the read end into `instance_id_read_data->buffer`. The async read is bounded to `INSTANCE_ID_BUFFER_SIZE - 1` bytes, leaving room for a terminating NUL — a correctly sized read into the fixed buffer. The write end fd is recorded in child_setup_data so it survives to the child.

The sandbox-expose paths (already validated by is_valid_expose) are turned into `--filesystem` arguments resolved under the caller's own instance_path. The sandbox-expose-fd / -fd-ro arrays each read an fd `handle`, bounds-check it against `[0, fds_len)` before `fds[handle]`, resolve the descriptor to a real path via `get_path_for_fd`, and add the corresponding filesystem argument. The read-only variant at 518-553 mirrors this. Again handle indices are validated before use, preventing OOB reads of the descriptor table.

The final argv assembly. `runtime_parts[1]` (runtime id) and `runtime_parts[3]` (branch) become `--runtime=`/`--runtime-version=` — this is where the missing length validation from line 101 bites. The commit ids, cwd, and the caller's program (arg_argv[0]) are each appended as a SINGLE `g_strdup_printf` argv element, so embedded spaces/quotes cannot split into extra options (no shell is involved — g_spawn takes an argv vector directly). The app ref `app_id/arch/branch` positional argument is added, then the caller's remaining program arguments arg_argv[1..] are appended as plain argv elements AFTER the ref, so they are arguments to the launched program, not flatpak options. Finally a NULL terminates the vector.

The child is launched via g_spawn_async_with_pipes using the assembled argv and `env` (the launcher/base environment, NOT the caller's confidential env values, which arrive via --env-fd). `child_setup_func` runs in the child to perform the fd dup2 dance from child_setup_data. On success a PidData record is created tracking the pid, the D-Bus sender, watch-bus/pids flags, and a child-watch source; it is inserted into the global `client_pid_data_hash`, and the method completes returning the pid to the caller. Error paths return a mapped D-Bus error.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
g_ptr_array_add (flatpak_argv, g_strdup_printf ("--runtime=%s", runtime_parts[1]));
  g_ptr_array_add (flatpak_argv, g_strdup_printf ("--runtime-version=%s", runtime_parts[3]));
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: `runtime_parts` comes from `g_strsplit (runtime_ref, "/", -1)` at line 101 and is only checked for `runtime_ref != NULL`, never for length. g_strsplit returns a NULL-terminated vector; if `runtime_ref` is malformed and contains fewer than four '/'-separated components, then `runtime_parts[1]` may be NULL and `runtime_parts[3]` reads PAST the NULL terminator — an out-of-bounds read of the string vector (and passing a wild/NULL pointer into g_strdup_printf's %s). For a two-element ref like `"runtime/foo"` the array is `{ "runtime", "foo", NULL }`; index [3] is off the end. The invariant the implementation must uphold is: validate `g_strv_length (runtime_parts) >= 4` (or the exact canonical shape `runtime/ID/ARCH/BRANCH`) BEFORE indexing [1] and [3]. Attacker-control is limited because runtime_ref lives in flatpak-generated `.flatpak-info` metadata rather than the direct D-Bus arguments, which keeps severity below the injection surface, but it is a real latent memory-safety defect present in the shown code.

This is the CWE-74 focal point. The caller (a sandboxed app) fully controls environment names and values, and the portal runs privileged. If these values were spliced onto the `flatpak run` command line (the historical bug), a value like `LD_PRELOAD=...` or an option-shaped string could inject code into the launcher `flatpak(1)` itself and escape the sandbox. Here the code defends correctly: the NAME is rejected if empty or contains '=' (lines 274/282, blocking `NAME=x\0INJECT=y` splitting), and the assembled blob is delivered out-of-band via a sealed memfd `--env-fd` (lines 365-381) rather than as visible argv, and is never merged into the launcher's own `env`. The invariant to keep is exactly this: caller env content must reach only the CHILD via the fd, never the launcher's environment or its command line. This is the mitigation for the flatpak-portal spawn environment-injection sandbox escape (CVE-2019-10063).

The caller controls `arg_argv`. The residual injection question is whether a caller string can become an extra `flatpak run` OPTION. It cannot in this construction: arg_argv[0] is embedded inside a single `--command=%s` element (spaces/quotes stay inside one argv slot because g_spawn uses an argv vector, not a shell), and arg_argv[1..] are appended AFTER the positional app-ref, so flatpak's option parser treats them as arguments to the launched program, not as launcher options. The invariant to preserve: caller argv must never be positioned before the app ref, and each caller string must remain one argv element (no shell, no `--command=` concatenation of multiple tokens). A regression that placed caller argv before the ref, or invoked via `/bin/sh -c`, would reopen argument injection (CWE-74).

The `handle` half of each `{uh}` entry is an index into the borrowed descriptor table `fds`. It is correctly bounds-checked against `[0, fds_len)` before `fds[handle]`, closing the out-of-bounds read. Note however that `dest_fd` (the 'u' member, an unsigned wire value read into a signed `gint32`) is NOT range-validated here: a caller can request an arbitrary/huge or negative destination fd number, which flows into `fd_map[i].to`/`.final` and drives dup2 target selection and the `max_fd` bump at line 249/220-221. The invariant the child-setup implementation must uphold is that it dup2s only to sane fd numbers and fails safely on absurd targets; the security impact is bounded (a caller can only rearrange fds within the child it is spawning), so this is a low-severity input-validation gap (CWE-20), not a memory-safety hole.

Two security-relevant NULL checks are expressed as `g_assert`. If the code were ever built with `G_DISABLE_ASSERT`, both assertions compile away, and a missing `app-info` (or a [Application] with no Name key) would lead to a NULL `app_info`/`app_id` being dereferenced downstream (e.g. `*app_id` at line 65, or app_id used in the argv at line 572) — a NULL-pointer dereference / crash of the privileged portal (CWE-476, denial of service). The invariant: the presence of app-info is a precondition guaranteed by the auth layer, so in normal builds this aborts loudly rather than misbehaving; still, safety-critical presence checks are better expressed as explicit `if (x == NULL) return error;` than as assertions that can be disabled.

A minor resource-hygiene concern, not a direct attacker primitive: once `child_setup_data.env_fd` (and later `child_setup_data.instance_id_fd`/`pipe_fds[1]` at 450) hold real descriptors, several later error paths (e.g. the spawn-failure return at 602-611) return without explicitly closing them, since they are plain ints in a stack struct rather than g_autofd. Repeated failed Spawn calls could leak descriptors in the long-lived portal process, a slow denial-of-service. The invariant: every early-return after a descriptor is stashed in child_setup_data must close it (or the object must own it via an autoptr). Confirm in the full function whether a cleanup label handles these; the shown returns do not.

summary: This is Flatpak's privileged org.freedesktop.portal.Flatpak Spawn handler, invoked by sandboxed apps to launch subprocesses via `flatpak run`. It is the hardened/fixed variant of a CWE-74 injection surface: caller-supplied environment values are validated (name non-empty, no '=') and delivered out-of-band through a sealed memfd `--env-fd` instead of the command line, caller argv is passed as discrete argv elements after the app ref (no shell, no option splicing), fd handle indices are bounds-checked before dereferencing the descriptor table, and flag/sandbox-flag masks are whitelisted — so the primary sandbox-escape vectors (CVE-2019-10063 env injection, CVE-2021-21261 fd reuse) are mitigated. The one concrete latent defect in the shown code is the unchecked indexing of `runtime_parts[1]`/`[3]` after g_strsplit without a length check (OOB read / NULL %s), plus minor issues: unranged `dest_fd`, security checks via g_assert, and descriptor leaks on error paths.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: handle_spawn signature: arg_cwd_path, arg_argv, arg_fds, arg_envs, arg_flags, arg_options, fd_list (lines 2-10)
reviewed_anchors.reason: These are the untrusted D-Bus arguments from a sandboxed caller; they are the CWE-74 injection surface feeding the flatpak run command line and environment.
Sentinel initialization to 'no fd'; correct, and prevents accidental fd 0 handling.
Descriptor table plus its length; used only after every handle index is bounds-checked against fds_len, so no OOB access.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: app_info via g_object_get_data + g_assert (56-57), app_id + g_assert (59-62)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Security-relevant NULL preconditions are enforced with g_assert, which is a no-op under G_DISABLE_ASSERT -> potential NULL deref (CWE-476).
Rejects non-flatpak callers; validated correctly.
Benign normalization; later added as a single --cwd= argv element.
Ensures a command exists before use; validated.
Whitelist rejects unknown flag bits; correct validation gate.
Only NULL-checked; feeds the unbounded g_strsplit indexing at 555-556.
Split length never validated before indexing [1]/[3]; OOB read / NULL %s (CWE-125/476).
Whitelisted; unknown sandbox flags rejected.
Requires an instance path before any expose; correct guard.
Each expose path validated (no absolute/.. traversal) before use; path-injection defense.
Allocation count and loop bound share n_fds, so fd_map[i] writes are in-bounds.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: fd handle bounds check handle>=fds_len||handle<0 then fds[handle] (195, 204)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Correctly bounds-checks the descriptor-table index before dereference; closes OOB read.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: dest_fd read as 'u' into gint32; fd_map[i].to/.final (193, 206-208)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Caller-controlled destination fd number is not range-validated (CWE-20), though impact is confined to the child it spawns.
Prevents dup2 clobbering a still-needed source fd; the fd-reuse hardening. No unchecked memory access.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: env name checks: var[0]=='\0' and strchr(var,'=') (274, 282)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Blocks empty names and '='-splitting in the env blob; correct validation.
The CWE-74 focal contract: caller env delivered via sealed memfd, not command line or launcher env. Correctly mitigated; documented as the primary finding.
Routes recorded env extra-args into the confidential blob with empty-name/missing-value checks; extra_args is metadata-sourced.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: supports & FLATPAK_SPAWN_SUPPORT_FLAGS_EXPOSE_PIDS gate; sender_pid1 from instance (391, 405-415)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Capability gate plus pid derived from caller's real instance, not a caller integer; not spoofable.
Read into the fixed buffer is capped at size-1 leaving room for NUL; correctly bounded.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: sandbox_expose_fd / _ro handle bounds check before fds[handle] (488, 525)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Handle validated against [0,fds_len) before dereference; same correct pattern as the main fd loop.
Argument-injection surface; contained because each caller string is one argv element placed after the app ref (no shell). Documented as mitigated CWE-74.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: child_setup_data.env_fd / instance_id_fd / pipe_fds[1] lifetime on error paths (377, 450, 602-611)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Plain-int descriptors not auto-closed; some error returns leak them (low-severity DoS).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: globals: supports, opt_verbose, client_pid_data_hash, child_setup_func, child_watch_died
reviewed_anchors.reason: Defined outside the shown code; their contracts (capability bits, verbose flag, pid table, dup2 callback) cannot be reviewed from this excerpt.
Uses an argv vector (no shell) and the base env; LEAVE_DESCRIPTORS_OPEN is deliberate per the dead-lock workaround comment. No injection via this call itself.

findings.title: Unvalidated g_strsplit result indexed at [1] and [3] (runtime ref)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read / NULL dereference (missing length check on split result)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0100_cwe74.c (this function) and implementation (file not provided) for the metadata source
findings.invariant: Before indexing runtime_parts[1] and [3], the code must verify the split produced the canonical 4-component form (e.g. g_strv_length(runtime_parts) >= 4, or reject non-'runtime/ID/ARCH/BRANCH' refs).
findings.failure_mode: If [Application] Runtime is malformed with fewer than four '/'-separated parts (e.g. "runtime/foo"), runtime_parts[1] may be NULL and runtime_parts[3] reads past the NULL-terminated vector's end, feeding a wild/NULL pointer to g_strdup_printf("%s") — an out-of-bounds read and possible crash of the privileged portal. Attacker-control is indirect (metadata is flatpak-generated), so this is a robustness/latent memory-safety bug rather than a direct remote primitive.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no earlier guard on runtime_parts length; add g_strv_length(runtime_parts) == 4 (or >= 4) validation with an error return before lines 555-556.

findings.title: Caller environment injection contained via sealed memfd --env-fd (CWE-74 mitigation)
findings.invariant: Caller-supplied environment names/values must never be spliced onto the flatpak(1) command line nor merged into the launcher's own environment; they may only reach the child out-of-band (via the memfd fd). Env names must reject empty and '=' to prevent record splitting.
findings.failure_mode: In the historical vulnerable design, caller env content placed on the launcher command line / environment (e.g. LD_PRELOAD) let a sandboxed app inject code into privileged flatpak(1) and escape the sandbox. This code neutralizes that by validating names and passing the whole env block through a sealed memfd referenced only by an integer --env-fd. A regression that reintroduced --env= on argv or merged caller env into `env` would reopen the escape.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no other code path appends caller env values to flatpak_argv or to `env`, and that flatpak_buffer_to_sealed_memfd_or_tmpfile actually seals the memfd so the child cannot be tricked; verify the child reads env only from env_fd.

findings.title: Caller argv passed as discrete argv elements after app ref (argument injection contained)
findings.anchor: g_strdup_printf("--command=%s", arg_argv[0]) and arg_argv[1..] appended after positional ref
findings.bug_class: Argument injection into flatpak run — mitigated by argv construction
findings.invariant: Caller argv must be passed as individual argv vector elements (never through a shell) and must be positioned AFTER the positional app-ref so flatpak's parser treats them as program arguments, not launcher options; arg_argv[0] must be a single --command= element.
findings.failure_mode: If caller argv were concatenated into a shell string, or placed before the app-ref, a caller could inject extra flatpak run options (e.g. --filesystem=host) and escape the sandbox. The current construction uses g_spawn's argv vector directly and orders caller args after the ref, so no option smuggling is possible.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm g_spawn_async_with_pipes is called with the argv vector (no G_SPAWN_ flags invoking a shell) and that nothing inserts caller strings before flatpak_argv's app-ref element.

findings.title: Caller-controlled destination fd number not range-validated
findings.bug_class: Missing input validation on target fd (unsigned wire value into signed int)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0100_cwe74.c and implementation (file not provided) for child_setup_func
findings.invariant: The child-setup dup2 logic must reject or fail safely on absurd/negative/huge dest_fd values; dest_fd is read from a 'u' member into a signed gint32 and is entirely caller-chosen.
findings.failure_mode: A caller can request an arbitrary destination fd (including values that reinterpret as negative, or very large). This flows into fd_map[i].to/.final and the max_fd computation; a badly chosen target could cause dup2 failures or fd churn in the child. Impact is confined to the child the caller is already spawning, so severity is low, but the value should still be sanity-checked in the child-setup implementation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check child_setup_func / the dup2 code path to confirm dest_fd is validated (non-negative, within a sane fd ceiling) before use, and that a bad value cannot affect fds outside the new child.

findings.title: Security-relevant NULL preconditions enforced with g_assert
findings.bug_class: NULL dereference if assertions are disabled
findings.invariant: The presence of app-info and the Name key must be enforced by a non-optimizable check before app_info/app_id are dereferenced (e.g. line 65 *app_id, line 572 app_id use).
findings.failure_mode: If built with G_DISABLE_ASSERT, these checks vanish; a missing app-info or absent [Application] Name would yield a NULL pointer dereferenced downstream, crashing the privileged portal (denial of service). In default builds g_assert aborts loudly, so exploitation beyond DoS is unlikely, but safety-critical presence checks should be explicit if-return guards.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the auth layer truly guarantees app-info is always attached, and consider replacing the asserts with explicit error returns so behavior is correct even under G_DISABLE_ASSERT.

audit_checklist: Verify runtime_parts length is validated (g_strv_length >= 4 / canonical runtime/ID/ARCH/BRANCH) before indexing [1] and [3] at lines 555-556.
Confirm no code path appends caller-supplied env names/values to flatpak_argv or to the launcher `env`; caller env must only reach the child via the sealed --env-fd memfd.
Confirm flatpak_buffer_to_sealed_memfd_or_tmpfile seals the memfd (F_SEAL_WRITE/SHRINK) so the env blob cannot be mutated after creation.
Confirm g_spawn_async_with_pipes runs the argv vector without any shell and that caller arg_argv[1..] are always positioned after the positional app-ref (no launcher-option injection).
Verify child_setup_func validates dest_fd/'to' fd targets (non-negative, sane ceiling) and that the dup2 ordering derived from the fd_map conflict pass cannot clobber a still-needed source fd.
Re-confirm every fd `handle` index is bounds-checked against fds_len before fds[handle] in all four fd loops (arg_fds, sandbox_expose_fd, sandbox_expose_fd_ro).
Check that error-return paths after child_setup_data.env_fd / instance_id_fd / pipe_fds[1] are populated close those descriptors to avoid leaks in the long-lived portal.
Confirm is_valid_expose rejects absolute paths and '..' traversal so sandbox-expose cannot escape the caller's instance_path.
Confirm app-info presence is guaranteed by the auth layer (or replace g_assert with explicit checks) so G_DISABLE_ASSERT builds cannot NULL-deref app_info/app_id.
